title: Malignant hyperthermia: ryanodine receptor Ca release
vignette: A 22-year-old man is undergoing surgical repair of an inguinal hernia. Anesthesia is induced and an endotracheal tube is placed. He then develops severe spasm of the masseter muscles, and there is a dramatic increase in expired carbon dioxide measured from the endotracheal tube. His core body temperature begins to increase and is now 38.9 C (102 F). Which of the following is the most likely underlying mechanism leading to elevated body temperature in this patient?

clues.text: Anesthesia is induced and an endotracheal tube is placed
clues.note: General anesthesia is the trigger setting. Inhaled volatile anesthetics (halothane, sevoflurane, isoflurane) and the depolarizing paralytic succinylcholine are the classic precipitants of malignant hyperthermia. The temporal link 'symptoms right after induction' is the key risk-factor clue.

clues.text: severe spasm of the masseter muscles
clues.note: Masseter (jaw) rigidity right after anesthesia is the earliest and most specific sign of malignant hyperthermia. Sustained muscle contracture results from uncontrolled sarcoplasmic calcium release. If you see jaw/masseter spasm after succinylcholine or a volatile agent, think MH first.

clues.text: dramatic increase in expired carbon dioxide
clues.note: Rising end-tidal CO2 despite ventilation is the earliest physiologic sign of MH. Massive ATP-driven calcium recycling and sustained contraction generate enormous CO2 and heat. This is the metabolic fingerprint of the hypermetabolic crisis.

clues.text: core body temperature begins to increase
clues.note: Hyperthermia is a LATE sign of MH (temp 38.9 / normal 36.5-37.5). The fever is downstream of the hypermetabolic muscle activity, not a hypothalamic set-point change. Treat immediately with dantrolene, which blocks the ryanodine receptor.

whatMatters: Strip the surgical story away: jaw rigidity + rising end-tidal CO2 + late fever right after anesthesia induction = malignant hyperthermia. The asked mechanism of the heat is uncontrolled calcium release into the myoplasm from a defective ryanodine receptor (RYR1) — answer C.
strategy: For 'mechanism of the fever' stems, decide first whether heat is from a hypothalamic set-point change (true fever: infection, pyrogens) or from peripheral heat OVER-production (MH, NMS, serotonin syndrome, thyroid storm). Post-anesthetic masseter rigidity + hypercarbia is MH = sarcoplasmic Ca overload, not a set-point shift.

options.text: Acute elevation of circulating free T4
Increased serotonergic activity in the CNS

eliminations.label: thyroid storm physiology — wrong trigger and tempo
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a surge of free T4/T3 drives thyroid storm with hyperthermia, tachycardia, and agitation. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: thyroid storm is precipitated by illness/surgery in a hyperthyroid patient over hours, not by anesthesia induction with masseter spasm and a CO2 spike. ✅ Correct if: the stem gave a goiter, prior Graves disease, suppressed TSH, or thyroidectomy stress.

eliminations.label: true fever (set-point shift) — but this is heat over-production
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: pyrogens (IL-1, IL-6, TNF, PGE2) raise the hypothalamic set point to make a true fever. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: MH heat comes from runaway skeletal-muscle metabolism, not a hypothalamic reset; there is no infection here and the masseter rigidity/hypercarbia point to the muscle. ✅ Correct if: the stem described infection or pyrogen-mediated fever with chills/shivering to reach a new set point.

eliminations.label: impaired heat dissipation — wrong category
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: failure of cutaneous vasodilation/sweating impairs heat LOSS (classic/exertional heat stroke, anticholinergic toxicity). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: MH is excess heat PRODUCTION from muscle, with hypercarbia and rigidity — not a dissipation problem, and there is no anticholinergic or environmental clue. ✅ Correct if: the stem emphasized a hot environment, anhidrosis, or anticholinergic 'dry as a bone' findings.

eliminations.label: serotonin syndrome — wrong drug class
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: excess CNS serotonin (SSRIs + MAOIs, linezolid, tramadol) causes hyperthermia, clonus, hyperreflexia, and agitation. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: there is no serotonergic drug and the picture is masseter rigidity + CO2 spike under anesthesia, which is MH. ✅ Correct if: the stem listed serotonergic agents with lower-limb clonus and hyperreflexia.

reasoning: Symptoms start right after anesthesia induction (volatile agent / succinylcholine)
Masseter rigidity + rising end-tidal CO2 = hypermetabolic muscle crisis
Late hyperthermia is downstream, not a set-point change
Defective RYR1 dumps Ca into the myoplasm → sustained contraction & heat → answer C
mechanism: Malignant hyperthermia is an autosomal dominant defect of the skeletal-muscle ryanodine receptor (RYR1, occasionally CACNA1S). Volatile anesthetics and succinylcholine trigger uncontrolled Ca2+ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum into the myoplasm. Sustained actin-myosin contraction consumes ATP, generating CO2, heat, and rigidity; ATP depletion causes rhabdomyolysis (hyperkalemia, myoglobinuria). Dantrolene blocks RYR1 to stop the calcium release and is the antidote.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Pharmacology > Anesthetics (malignant hyperthermia): RYR1 mutation, triggers = inhaled volatile anesthetics + succinylcholine, signs (masseter rigidity, hypercarbia, rhabdo, hyperthermia), antidote = dantrolene. Cross-link to the muscle excitation-contraction coupling diagram.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: The question asks the mechanism of the HEAT. Separate 'set-point fever' from 'peripheral heat over-production.' Post-induction rigidity + CO2 spike = MH = sarcoplasmic Ca overload.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on ryanodine receptor, sarcoplasmic reticulum, myoplasm, hypercarbia, excitation-contraction coupling? Tell me which and I'll define it.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Each wrong option is a different hyperthermia syndrome (thyroid storm, true fever, heat stroke, serotonin syndrome). The post-anesthetic masseter spasm + CO2 spike pins it to MH; don't let 'fever = set point' reflex pull you to B.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: You picked C and were right — lock in the trigger list (volatile agents + succinylcholine) and dantrolene as the single must-know antidote.

keywords.front: Masseter rigidity + rising end-tidal CO2 after anesthesia

keywords.back: Ryanodine receptor RYR1 (Ca release from SR)

firstAid.detail: Pharmacology > Anesthetics: RYR1 mutation, volatile-anesthetic/succinylcholine trigger, dantrolene treatment, autosomal dominant inheritance.
firstAid.location: Pharmacology > Anesthetics: RYR1 mutation, volatile-anesthetic/succinylcholine trigger, dantrolene treatment, autosomal dominant inheritance.

firstAid.detail: Physiology/Musculoskeletal: T-tubule depolarization → DHP receptor → RYR1 → SR Ca2+ release → troponin C → contraction; SERCA reuptake.
firstAid.location: Physiology/Musculoskeletal: T-tubule depolarization → DHP receptor → RYR1 → SR Ca2+ release → troponin C → contraction; SERCA reuptake.

firstAid.detail: Pharm/Psych: thyroid storm (free T4), true fever (PGE2 set point), heat stroke (impaired dissipation), serotonin syndrome (clonus), NMS (D2 blockade, lead-pipe rigidity).
firstAid.location: Pharm/Psych: thyroid storm (free T4), true fever (PGE2 set point), heat stroke (impaired dissipation), serotonin syndrome (clonus), NMS (D2 blockade, lead-pipe rigidity).

memoryHook: MH = 'Muscle Heat': RYR1 dumps calcium → jaw locks, CO2 climbs, temp rises. Dantrolene 'dams' the ryanodine channel.
